materials.title: Materials
materials.hero_text: Browse 152 natural stones across 6 types — marble, onyx, limestone, travertine, granite, and quartzite — from 10 countries.
material.price_note: All prices are ex-works (FOB origin port). Freight, insurance, and import duties are not included.
collections.title: Collections
collections.hero_text: Each collection is a narrative — stones selected for their visual dialogue, shared origins, or complementary textures.
projects.title: Projects
projects.hero_text: Residential, hospitality, cultural, and commercial — each project tells a story of material integrity and collaboration with the world's leading architects.
heritage.title: Our Heritage
heritage.hero_text: Before there was a company, there was a woman — and her name was DIJA. This is our story.
heritage.section_name_text1: Before there was a company, there was a woman.
heritage.section_name_text2: Khadija — the grandmother of our founders — was not in the stone trade. She didn't read geological surveys or negotiate quarry contracts. But she understood something that no textbook can teach: that beauty, when built on honesty, lasts forever. She raised a family on the belief that a person's word is their most valuable material — stronger than steel, more enduring than stone itself. Her vision of integrity and family wasn't something she lectured about; it was something she lived, in every room she kept, every guest she welcomed, and every decision she made with quiet, unshakable grace.
heritage.section_name_text3: When it came time to name this company, there was only ever one name that felt right. DIJA is her name, carried forward. It is our promise that everything we build, source, and ship will honor the values she instilled in us — beauty with integrity, ambition with honesty, and business conducted the way she conducted her life: with her word as her bond.
heritage.section_name_text4: This is the story of how her vision became the foundation of a company built to serve the world's most discerning architects, developers, and homeowners.
heritage.section_genesis_text: Before the first quarry was ever visited, there was a drafting table. The founders of DIJA were raised around an architect — their mother — who was entrusted with massive, landmark projects. Their playground was her studio, where they watched her and her peers debate spatial flow, structural engineering, and the poetry of natural light for hours on end. Spread across the worn oak tables were treasures from the earth: the icy purity of White Calacatta and the volcanic intensity of Red Rosso Levanto. They learned to read these marbles by their fossils and mineral veins before they could pronounce their names, understanding that each stone carried a distinct emotional vocabulary. But it was limestone that captured their souls. Placing a palm against a limestone sample, they felt a profound tranquility — a grounding sensation that no other material could replicate, as if they were touching compressed time itself. Beyond the stone, they fell in love with architecture in its entirety: the mathematics of perfect proportions, the harmony between a facade and its landscape, the texture of light on matte surfaces, and the biophilic connection between a building and its inhabitants. They realized that stone was not the hero, but the silent partner to the architect's vision — and they were destined to become its most devoted guardians.
heritage.section_trust_text: In 2017, our passion crystallized into a profession. We immersed ourselves in the heart of Turkey's limestone trade — specifically the storied quarries of Finike and Antalya, regions whose stone has shaped Mediterranean architecture for centuries. But we did not simply buy blocks. We bought into relationships. We learned to read the stone: how the angle of extraction could change a slab's soul; how the right block in the wrong hands could be wasted; and how the right block in the right hands could become a masterpiece. These weren't transactional relationships. They were friendships forged over endless cups of tea in quarry offices, late nights discussing block yields, and the kind of mutual respect that only comes from years of showing up and keeping promises. We learned that in an industry so often plagued by opacity, transparency is the ultimate luxury.
heritage.section_decade: A Decade of Curating the Earth
heritage.section_decade_text: What followed was a relentless pursuit — not of volume, but of distinction. We traveled across the Mediterranean and far beyond it, building relationships with family-owned quarries from the highlands of Brazil to the ancient stone belts of India. Brazil taught us about drama and movement in exotic granites. India taught us patience and precision in processing. The Mediterranean taught us heritage and timelessness. In an industry flooded with middlemen and anonymous suppliers, we chose a harder, slower path — one built on direct relationships with the families who own and operate the quarries themselves. This is the lesson Khadija taught us at home, translated into business: Relationships built on honesty outperform every shortcut. When you know the hands that extract the stone, you can trust the integrity of what they send you.
heritage.section_tunisia_text: In 2022, we brought our expertise home to Tunisia. We saw what much of the world hadn't yet noticed — that Tunisian natural stone possesses a rare combination of color, density, and character placing it on a trajectory to rival even the legendary marbles of Italy. We weren't just expanding a business; we were betting on a homeland's hidden treasure. This chapter wasn't built on contracts alone — it was built on family, just as Khadija would have wanted. Our partnership — uniting expertise from Qatar, Tunisia, and Turkey — stands as a testament to her belief that trust between people who would never let each other down is the foundation of greatness. Today, our portfolio expands across the Mediterranean — supplying refined natural stone to private residences, luxury resorts in the Maldives, and exclusive villas throughout the GCC. Each project is a new opportunity to prove that beauty and integrity can — and should — travel together.
heritage.section_today_text: Today, DIJA operates from its headquarters in Izmir, with branches reaching across Canada, Italy, Qatar, and Tunisia. This footprint gives us access to one of the most diverse portfolios of Mediterranean-mined natural stone available anywhere in the world — granites, marbles, limestones, and travertines, each sourced directly from the family quarries we've come to know and trust. For our clients — architects, developers, and designers who share our obsession with material integrity — DIJA represents something increasingly rare: a supplier who understands stone not as inventory, but as legacy. We know the geological story behind every block. We know the families behind every quarry. We know that the projects you build with our stone will outlive all of us — which is exactly why we refuse to compromise on quality, transparency, or honesty at any step of the journey.
heritage.section_future_text1: Our ambition is to become one of the most trusted natural stone suppliers in the world — not by being the biggest, but by being the most reliable. We want architects in Doha, developers in Toronto, and designers in Milan to know that when they see the name DIJA on a delivery manifest, they are getting stone sourced with integrity, vetted with expertise, and delivered with the same care Khadija put into everything she touched. Because at the end of the day, DIJA isn't just a stone trading company. It is a grandmother's values, carried across oceans and generations, embedded into every slab we ship. It is proof that beauty built on honesty doesn't erode — it endures, just like the stone itself.
heritage.section_future_text2: This is our story. We would be honored to help write the next chapter of yours.
quarries.title: Partner Quarries
quarries.hero_text: We don't source from catalogues. Our team visits 30+ family-owned quarries across 10 countries, selects every block by hand, and maintains relationships measured in decades — not transactions.
quarries.ethos_title:
We evaluate quarries the way
architects evaluate stone:
by what's beneath the surface.
quarries.ethos_text: Before we work with a quarry, our technical team spends 3–5 days on-site evaluating block yield, vein consistency, colour uniformity, and structural integrity. We benchmark test blocks for gang-saw compatibility, calibrate moisture profiles, and review environmental compliance documentation — including rehabilitation bonding and social responsibility commitments. A quarry earns our business not through pricing, but through repeatable quality and transparent operations.
quarries.methods_title:
How the stone
comes out of the ground.
quarries.block_note: Ranges represent commercially available block sizes. Actual dimensions vary by quarry and deposit geometry.
quarries.field_note_1: The dust settles differently in a marble quarry. It's fine, almost chalky — it gets into everything, but it never feels dirty. Just old. I was in the Apuan Alps last October, standing on a bench that had been worked continuously since the Romans. The quarry master — third generation — pointed to a face and said, "My grandfather cut that wall. My father cut beside it. I'm still following the same vein." He wasn't being sentimental. He was telling me that the stone dictates the schedule, not the other way around.
quarries.field_note_2: That's what we look for. Not the biggest quarry or the cheapest block, but the quarry master who can read the geology in the colour of the mud on his boots. That knowledge is irreplaceable. You can't scale it, you can't automate it, and you can't fake it in a PDF.
quarries.footprint_text: Our sourcing footprint spans the Mediterranean basin, the Indian subcontinent, and the Brazilian shield. Each region selected for its geological distinctiveness — no two countries in our portfolio produce interchangeable stone.
quarries.footprint_title:
From the Mediterranean
to the New World.
quarries.custom_title:
Looking for a specific
origin, block size, or colour?
quarries.custom_text: Tell us your project parameters and we'll identify the quarry that can deliver. We don't stock generic inventory — every block we move is selected for a specific purpose.
quarries.geology_turkey: The Anatolian plateau spans Triassic metamorphics (~230 Ma) in Afyon and Marmara Island, Miocene lacustrine deposits (~15 Ma) in Burdur and Antalya, and Pliocene-Pleistocene travertine terraces (~2 Ma) in Denizli — one of the most geologically diverse stone regions on earth.
quarries.geology_italy: The Apuan Alps are a Jurassic marine basin (~180 Ma) — compressed plankton skeletons metamorphosed into the world's most celebrated marble. Veneto yields Cretaceous limestone with distinctive fossil inclusions. Sicily's Miocene calcarenites (~15 Ma) produce warm Perlato di Sicilia.
quarries.geology_greece: Greek marbles are predominantly Triassic (~230 Ma) — recrystallized carbonate platforms from the Tethys Ocean. Thassos Island produces the whitest marble in the world due to its exceptionally low iron oxide content (<0.02% Fe₂O₃).
quarries.geology_spain: Spain's stone belts span Triassic (~230 Ma) marbles in Almería, Jurassic (~160 Ma) in Murcia, Cretaceous (~120 Ma) in the Basque Country, and Miocene (~15 Ma) calcarenites in Alicante — each producing distinctly different colour palettes.
quarries.geology_france: French limestones range from Jurassic (~170 Ma) oolitic and shell beds in Burgundy to Cretaceous (~100 Ma) in Languedoc. The Pyrenees yield a unique Cretaceous marble (Noir des Pyrénées) from deep marine anoxic deposits.
quarries.geology_portugal: The Estremoz marble belt (Alentejo) is a Cambrian-Silurian metamorphic sequence (~400 Ma) — among the oldest commercial marbles in Europe. Portuguese limestone (Moca Creme) is Cretaceous (~100 Ma) from the Lisbon region.
quarries.geology_tunisia: Tunisia's stone resources span Jurassic (~160 Ma) marbles in Chemtou and Cretaceous (~100 Ma) limestones in Thala and Kesra. The Chemtou deposit (Giallo Antico) was quarried by the Romans for monuments across the empire.
quarries.geology_india: Indian marble (Rajasthan) is Precambrian (~800 Ma) — recrystallized from carbonate platforms of the ancient Aravalli range. Indian granites are among the oldest exposed rocks on earth at ~2,500 Ma (Archean).
quarries.geology_brazil: Brazilian granites and quartzites are Precambrian (~2,000 Ma to ~1,500 Ma) — among the hardest, most durable stones in commercial use. The Bahia region yields unique blue granites coloured by sodic amphibole inclusions.
quarries.geology_iran: Iran holds some of the world's richest onyx and travertine deposits — banded calcite laid down by mineral hot springs from the Neogene to the present around Azarshahr and Mahallat, alongside Cretaceous limestones and granite massifs of the Zagros belt near Isfahan.
quarries.extraction_turkey: Diamond wire for marble; chain saw and wire for limestone; diamond wire and chain saw for travertine benches.
quarries.extraction_italy: Diamond wire sawing for marble blocks; chain saw cutting for limestone benches. Hand-selection at the bench face is standard practice.
quarries.extraction_greece: Diamond wire extraction with helix cutting for vein-following. Open-pit bench mining at 8–12 m bench heights.
quarries.extraction_spain: Diamond wire for marble; chain saw and diamond wire for limestone. Deeper quarries (40–80 m benches) require multi-bench layouts.
quarries.extraction_france: Chain saw cutting for limestone (Pierre de Bourgogne, Massangis); diamond wire for harder marbles.
quarries.extraction_portugal: Diamond wire for marble — Estremoz quarries are among the deepest in Europe (60–100 m benches). Chain saw for limestone.
quarries.extraction_tunisia: Diamond wire for marble; mechanical excavators and chain saw for softer limestone.
quarries.extraction_india: Drill-and-blast for granite blocks with diamond wire secondary cutting. Diamond wire for marble. Gang saw processing at mill yards.
quarries.extraction_brazil: Drill-and-blast primary breakage, diamond wire secondary cutting. Block sizes are larger than Mediterranean norms due to massive deposit geometry.
quarries.extraction_iran: Diamond wire for travertine and marble benches; careful chain-saw and wire extraction for fragile onyx lenses, which occur as pockets rather than continuous beds.
quarries.blocks_spain: Crema Marfil / Marrón Emperador: 2.5–2.8 × 1.3–1.6 × 1.1–1.4 m, 14–18 t. Nero Marquina: 2.4–2.7 × 1.2–1.5 × 1.1–1.3 m, 13–16 t.
quarries.blocks_iran: Travertine: 2.4–2.8 × 1.3–1.6 × 1.0–1.2 m, 12–16 t. Onyx: smaller irregular blocks, typically 3–8 t, selected individually for banding and translucency.
quarries.ports_turkey: İzmir (Alsancak), Bandırma, Antalya, Mersin — all within 150–300 km of major quarry districts.
quarries.ports_italy: Carrara (direct wharf access for marble blocks), La Spezia, Savona, Venice, Palermo.
quarries.ports_greece: Kavala, Thessaloniki, Volos — all with dedicated block-handling facilities.
quarries.ports_spain: Alicante, Valencia, Barcelona — all within 2 hours of main quarry districts.
quarries.ports_brazil: Vitória (major granite export hub), Salvador, Santos.
quarries.ports_iran: Bandar Abbas; overland via Türkiye to Mersin and Izmir for Mediterranean consolidation.
quarries.note_turkey: Turkey is our home market and deepest sourcing territory. Our team is on-site at active quarries weekly.
quarries.note_italy: Our longest-standing partnerships — some spanning 15+ years. We visit a minimum of 4 times per year.
quarries.note_greece: Greek white marbles are benchmarked for whiteness index (L* > 92). We reject blocks where colour deviates.
quarries.note_spain: Crema Marfil is the highest-volume stone we move from Spain. We grade exclusively in A/B qualities.
quarries.note_france: French limestones are specified by architects for their consistent bedding plane structure. Our Pierre de Bourgogne selection focuses on frost-resistant grades.
quarries.note_portugal: Estremoz marble's fine grain structure makes it ideal for sculptural and architectural millwork. We track block colour consistency batch-to-batch.
quarries.note_tunisia: Tunisia is our growth frontier. We invested heavily in local partnerships from 2022 — bringing Tunisian stone to international specifications.
quarries.note_india: We source only from quarries with valid environmental clearance and labour compliance certifications. Granite block selection is done at the yard after washing.
quarries.note_brazil: Brazilian quartzite requires specialized processing tooling (diamond-impregnated blades). We work exclusively with processors who certify blade pressure and feed rate.
quarries.note_iran: Iranian onyx and travertine are graded at the yard for banding and translucency before purchase — we consolidate shipments through our Izmir facility.
quarries.method_1_desc: A diamond-impregnated steel cable loops through drilled pilot holes and cuts through the rock mass at 6–12 m²/hour. Produces clean block faces with minimal micro-fracturing — essential for high-yield marble extraction.
quarries.method_2_desc: A hydraulically powered cutting arm with tungsten-carbide teeth — similar to a oversized chainsaw — kerfs vertical and horizontal cuts in softer stones. Preferred for limestone benches where diamond wire would overheat.
quarries.method_3_desc: Precisely angled boreholes charged with low-vibration explosives to separate massive granite blocks from the bedrock. Modern micro-delay detonators control fracture propagation to within centimetres.
quarries.method_4_desc: A specialized diamond wire technique that follows the natural vein orientation — rather than cutting across it — to maximize the commercial value of figure-veined stones like Calacatta and Statuario.
sustainability.title: Sustainability
sustainability.hero_text: Natural stone is one of the lowest-carbon building materials available. It requires no kiln-firing, no chemical synthesis, and no petroleum-based binders. A stone slab is simply the earth, cut and finished.
sustainability.stat_92: of our tonnage is shipped by sea, reducing carbon per ton-mile by 84% compared to road freight.
sustainability.stat_100: of quarry waste (scrap, slurry, offcuts) is recycled into aggregate, road base, and landscaping material.
sustainability.stat_30: family quarries we partner with, all operating under local environmental regulations and rehabilitation plans.
sustainability.philosophy_title:
Stone doesn't need
to be manufactured.
It needs to be respected.
sustainability.philosophy_text: Every block we move carries the weight of the mountain it came from — and our responsibility to leave that mountain and its people better than we found them.
sustainability.pillar_carbon_text: We prioritize sea freight — 84% less CO₂ per ton-mile than road transport. Our partner quarries in Turkey and Tunisia operate on hybrid solar-grid energy, and we offset the remainder through verified reforestation programs in the Mediterranean basin.
sustainability.pillar_water_text: Water is the lifeblood of stone processing. All of our partner facilities use closed-loop filtration systems — recycling up to 95% of water used in gang-saw cutting and surface finishing. Slurry is pressed into filter cakes and repurposed as raw material for concrete block production.
sustainability.pillar_waste_text: Nothing we extract goes to waste. Overburden becomes aggregate for road construction. Offcuts are transformed into tiles, vanities, and landscaping products. Slurry and sludge are processed into building materials. Even our wooden crates are sourced from FSC-certified forests and reused when possible.
sustainability.pillar_community_text: Every quarry we work with is family-owned and locally operated. We ensure fair wages, safe working conditions, and long-term rehabilitation plans that return quarry sites to their natural state. Our direct relationships eliminate the exploitation often hidden in multi-tier supply chains.
sustainability.pillar_community_target: 100% of partner quarries with published rehabilitation plans.
sustainability.step_source_text: Blocks selected for maximum yield, minimal waste. Quarries operate under local environmental regulations with binding rehabilitation commitments.
sustainability.step_cut_text: Water-efficient gang saws and diamond wire technology minimize energy use and slurry generation. 95% of process water is recycled.
sustainability.step_finish_text: Low-VOC sealers and natural finishes. No chemical brightening or resin filling. The stone's surface is simply revealed, not altered.
sustainability.step_ship_text: Carbon-optimized sea freight with minimal, FSC-certified packaging. Consolidated containers reduce per-slab transport emissions.
sustainability.step_return_text: At end of life, natural stone returns to the earth — crushed into aggregate, ground into soil amendment, or repurposed as architectural fill.
sustainability.milestone_2015: First partner quarry transitions to solar-powered operations, reducing grid dependency by 60%.
sustainability.milestone_2018: Zero-waste certification achieved across all Turkish partner facilities. 100% of quarry byproducts now recycled or repurposed.
sustainability.milestone_2020: Closed-loop water filtration implemented across all processing facilities. Water recycling rate reaches 95%.
sustainability.milestone_2022: First comprehensive carbon footprint audit published. Baseline established for Scope 1, 2, and 3 emissions across the supply chain.
sustainability.milestone_2024: Blockchain-based traceability pilot launched. Every slab now carries a digital passport documenting its origin, processing, and carbon impact.
sustainability.milestone_2026: Net-zero pathway published. Committed to 50% reduction in Scope 1 & 2 emissions by 2030, with full offset of residual emissions.
sustainability.quote_text: "The most sustainable building material is the one that has been here for 200 million years — and will still be here 200 million years from now."
sustainability.cta_title:
Specify stone that
leaves a lighter footprint.
sustainability.cta_text: Every project starts with a conversation. Tell us what you're building, and we'll help you choose the stone that meets your aesthetic, your budget, and your sustainability goals.
journal.title: Journal
contact.title: Contact Us
contact.hero_text: Six offices and four trusted partners across nine countries — we're where you need us to be.
contact.form_message: Tell us about your project, request a sample, or ask for a quote...
contact.form_success: Thank you! Your message has been sent.
contact.form_error: Failed to send. Please try again.
contact.response_text: We respond within 24 hours, typically within 4 hours during business hours.
trade.title: Trade Program
trade.hero_text: Competitive pricing, direct sourcing, dedicated support, and trade credit terms for qualified professionals in architecture, design, and construction.
trade.philosophy_text: We don't measure our trade program by the number of partners we sign. We measure it by the quality of the relationships we build. A partnership with DIJA means direct access to quarry-origin pricing, dedicated technical support from people who have stood on the bench, and a supply chain built on transparency — not volume targets or sales quotas. We would rather work with fifty partners who share our values than five hundred who don't.
trade.philosophy_text2: If you specify stone because you care about how it was sourced, where it came from, and who selected it — you belong here.
trade.form_text: This form helps us understand who you are and what you value in a stone partner. We review every application personally — there is no automated approval. Expect to hear from our trade team within 2 business days.
trade.field_referral: How did you hear about DIJA?
trade.field_referral_ph: e.g. industry referral, trade show, online search
trade.field_values: What matters most to you in a stone supplier?
trade.field_project: Tell us about a recent project where material selection was critical
trade.field_project_ph: Describe the project, the materials you considered, and why stone was the right choice...
trade.field_notes: Anything else you'd like us to know?
trade.field_notes_ph: Your practice, current projects, or specific requirements...
trade.success_text: Thank you for your interest in the DIJA Trade Program. Our team will review your application and contact you within 2 business days.
trade.success_contact: If you have an urgent requirement, please reach us directly at
trade.error_alert: Could not submit application. Please email us at trade@dijastones.com.
trade.criteria_title:
Partners who see stone
the way we do.
trade.criteria_integrity_text: You specify stone because it's the right material for the project — not because it's trending. You understand that natural variation is not a defect but a signature.
trade.criteria_transparency_text: You want to know where the stone came from, who cut it, and how it got to you. You ask questions about quarry practices, not just pricing.
trade.criteria_craft_text: You understand that natural stone demands skilled hands — at the quarry, at the processing yard, and on the installation site. You don't rush the material.
trade.criteria_vision_text: You're building a practice, a portfolio, a reputation — not chasing a single project. You value a supplier who will be there for the next job, and the one after that.
trade.criteria_quality_text: You would rather specify the right stone at a fair price than compromise on material quality for a marginal cost saving. You know that cheap stone is the most expensive mistake.
trade.criteria_sustainability_text: You care about the environmental and social impact of your material choices. You look for partners who can document their practices, not just claim them.
trade.benefits_text: Exclusive pricing, priority access to new arrivals, and a dedicated account manager who understands the demands of your project pipeline.
trade.benefit_pricing_text: No middlemen, no markups. Our pricing reflects what we pay at the quarry gate, plus logistics and handling — nothing more.
trade.benefit_priority_text: First sight of new block arrivals, exclusive access to limited-stock materials, and advance notice of price movements.
trade.benefit_manager_text: A single point of contact who knows your project history, your preferred stones, and your specifications — no explaining yourself twice.
trade.benefit_sampling_text: Bespoke sample kits, on-site material presentations, and technical consultation on stone selection, fabrication, and installation.
trade.benefit_credit_text: Net-30 and project-based payment terms for qualified partners. No prepayment hassles on confirmed orders.
trade.benefit_support_text: On-site visits for large-scale projects, installation guidance, and after-delivery support. We don't disappear after the invoice is paid.
trade.qualifies_1: Architecture & interior design firms committed to material integrity
trade.qualifies_2: General contractors & developers who value traceable supply chains
trade.qualifies_3: Stone fabricators & installers with verifiable quality standards
trade.qualifies_4: Hospitality & retail buyers specifying at project scale
trade.qualifies_5: Landscape architects working with natural stone
trade.not_ready_title:
Explore our materials
at your own pace.
trade.not_ready_text: Browse our full catalogue of 152 stones, each with technical specifications, application guidance, and origin details.

proforma.title: Proforma Builder
proforma.signin_text: You need to sign in to create and manage proformas. It only takes a minute.
proforma.signin_reason_2: Track all your proformas in one place
proforma.hero_text: Select stones from our library, choose premade sizes or custom dimensions, and receive a detailed proforma with container-based shipping estimates and payment terms.
proforma.items_note: Each stone item lets you select grade (Standard / Premium), category, size, thickness, finish, and quantity. The price breakdown updates automatically.
proforma.items_empty_text: Select a stone from the dropdown, choose size options, and enter the total area needed. Add multiple items to compare pricing.
proforma.payment_lc_text: Valid for established clients with prime bank confirmation
proforma.origin_ports_note: Select stones in Step 2 to see origin ports.
proforma.shipping_note: Shipping is calculated per 20ft container (9 m³ volume). Capacity = 900 / thickness (cm). Example: 2 cm = 450 m², 3 cm = 300 m², 1 cm = 900 m².
proforma.notes_ph: Special requirements, delivery instructions, or any additional information...
proforma.review_text: Please review all details before submitting. You can go back to any step to make changes.
proforma.send_confirm: Send this proforma to your verified email?
proforma.select_prompt: Select a stone, size, and area
proforma.incoterm_ddp_note: Need Delivered at Place / Delivered Duty Paid? Contact our trade team — these require case-by-case customs arrangements in your country.
proforma.notes_for_team_ph: Any special instructions, delivery constraints, etc.
proforma.err_client_required: Client name and email are required.
proforma.err_items_required: Add at least one stone item with an area.
proforma_view.title: Proforma Invoice
proforma_pdf.shipping_estimate_note: * Transportation costs are estimated and may vary by destination and market rates at the time of shipment.
proforma_pdf.payment_methods: Payment Methods Accepted: Bank Wire Transfer (T/T), Confirmed Irrevocable L/C at Sight, Cash Against Documents (subject to approval)
proforma_pdf.tnc_1: 1. This proforma is valid for 14 days from the issue date.
proforma_pdf.tnc_2: 2. Prices are in USD. Base prices are for 2 cm thickness, Large Slab category, Polished finish. Size, thickness, and finish multipliers apply as per selected options.
proforma_pdf.tnc_3: 3. Container count is calculated based on 20ft container capacity: 450 m² (2 cm), 300 m² (3 cm), 600 m² (1 cm). Final count may vary.
proforma_pdf.tnc_4: 4. Natural stone is a product of nature. Color, veining, and texture variations are inherent characteristics and do not constitute defects.
proforma_pdf.tnc_5: 5. Final dimensions and quantities are subject to ±5%% tolerance per industry standards.
proforma_pdf.tnc_6: 6. Packing: Standard seaworthy wooden crates suitable for container shipping (included in price).
proforma_pdf.tnc_7: 7. Insurance: Marine insurance is recommended for all shipments and can be arranged at 0.5%% of the total invoice value.
proforma_pdf.tnc_8: 8. Claims for damage or shortage must be reported within 7 days of receipt.
proforma_pdf.generated_notice: This is a computer-generated document. No signature is required. — %s
proforma_pdf.footer_address: DIJA Natural Stone — 10031 Sok. No:14, AOSB, Çiğli 35620 — İzmir, Türkiye

catalogue.title: DIJA Natural Stone Catalogue
catalogue.why_dija_p1: You’re choosing natural stone for a project that matters. We understand the weight of that decision.
catalogue.why_dija_p2: DIJA was built around a simple principle: you deserve to know exactly what you’re specifying. Every stone in this catalogue is sourced directly from quarries we know personally — with a team that handles grading, logistics, and quality control at every step. We label by geological formation, not marketing name. We publish real numbers — absorption, density, compressive strength — not brochure numbers.
catalogue.why_dija_p3: Five offices across three continents. A catalogue built for architects, by people who respect what architects do.
catalogue.why_dija_p4: The stone you see is the stone you get. That’s the commitment.
catalogue.about_p1: Before there was a company, there was a woman — and her name was DIJA. Khadija, the grandmother of our founders, taught that beauty built on honesty lasts forever. Her vision became the standard of a company that serves the world’s most discerning architects, developers, and homeowners.
catalogue.about_p2: Today, DIJA operates from Izmir, with branches across Canada, Italy, Qatar, and Tunisia. Our portfolio spans marble, limestone, travertine, granite, and quartzite — each sourced from quarries we know and trust across the Mediterranean, Brazil, and India.
catalogue.about_val1_desc: We visit every quarry ourselves. If we wouldn’t put it in our own home, we won’t put it in our catalogue.
catalogue.about_val2_desc: We sort by colour, veining, and structural soundness — not by what the market will bear. Every slab is inspected before it ships.
catalogue.about_val3_desc: Natural stone took millions of years to form. We treat it with the care it deserves, from block to final delivery.
catalogue.about_val4_desc: Geological age, compressive strength, water absorption, density — we publish the real numbers, not the “brochure” numbers.
catalogue.ref_app_lead: %d stones · 6 types · matching each application to the right stone — ✓ recommended   ⚠ use with care   ✘ not recommended
catalogue.ref_care_lead: Natural stone is durable but porous. Proper care preserves its appearance and structural integrity for decades. These guidelines apply across all stone types.
catalogue.ref_care_card1_text: Use pH-neutral stone cleaners and a soft cloth or microfiber mop. Avoid vinegar, lemon, bleach, ammonia, or any acidic or abrasive cleaners. Wipe spills immediately — especially wine, coffee, citrus, and oil.
catalogue.ref_care_card2_text: All natural stone benefits from sealing. Frequency depends on porosity and traffic. Limestone and travertine require more frequent sealing; granite and quartzite less. Test annually with a water drop — if it darkens the stone, re-seal.
catalogue.ref_care_card3_text: Identify the stain type: organic (coffee, wine) use hydrogen peroxide; oil-based use mineral spirits; ink/marker use acetone. Always test in an inconspicuous area first. For deep stains, consult a professional.
catalogue.glossary_absorption_desc: The ability of stone to absorb water, expressed as a percentage of dry weight. Lower values indicate better resistance to staining and freeze-thaw damage.
catalogue.glossary_calcite_desc: A crystalline form of calcium carbonate (CaCO₃), the primary mineral in marble, limestone, and travertine. Reacts with acids — this is what causes etching.
catalogue.glossary_comp_strength_desc: The maximum load a stone can withstand before failure, measured in megapascals (MPa). Higher values indicate greater structural capacity for load-bearing applications.
catalogue.glossary_crosscut_desc: Travertine cut perpendicular (cross-cut) or parallel (vein-cut) to the bedding plane. Cross-cut reveals filled cavities as dots; vein-cut emphasises continuous banding.
catalogue.glossary_density_desc: Mass per unit volume, typically kg/m³. Higher density generally correlates with greater durability, strength, and polishability.
catalogue.glossary_etching_desc: A dull, whitish mark left on calcite-based stones by acidic substances (lemon, wine, vinegar). Unlike a stain, etching is surface corrosion and cannot be wiped away — it must be re-honed or re-polished.
catalogue.glossary_filled_desc: Refers to travertine where natural surface cavities are filled with cement or resin (filled) for a smooth finish, or left open (unfilled) for a rustic, textured appearance.
catalogue.glossary_fossiliferous_desc: Containing visible fossil remains — shells, coral, or marine organisms. Common in limestone and some marbles, adding character and a record of geological history.
catalogue.glossary_honed_desc: A matte to satin finish with low light reflection. Achieved by stopping the polishing process before the final grit. Conceals wear and etching better than polished.
catalogue.glossary_leather_desc: A low-sheen, slightly textured finish with a tactile feel. Achieved by brushing with diamond-tipped nylon brushes. Resists fingerprints and hides wear well.
catalogue.glossary_mohs_desc: A 1–10 scale of mineral hardness (talc=1, diamond=10). Marble, onyx, limestone, travertine ~3–4; granite ~6–7; quartzite ~7. Higher numbers resist scratching better.
catalogue.glossary_polished_desc: A glossy, mirror-like finish achieved through fine-grit abrasion (up to 3,000+ grit). Maximises colour depth and veining contrast but shows etching and scratches more readily.
catalogue.glossary_porosity_desc: The volume of void space within a stone, affecting water absorption and stain susceptibility. Lower porosity = greater stain resistance.
catalogue.glossary_sealing_desc: Application of a penetrating or topical sealer to reduce porosity and protect against stains. Not permanent — reapplication is needed periodically based on stone type and wear.
catalogue.glossary_slip_desc: Surface Roughness Value measured by the Pendulum Test. Higher SRV = better grip when wet. UK guidelines: SRV ≥36 for pedestrian areas, ≥25 for barefoot areas.
catalogue.glossary_translucency_desc: The ability of stone to transmit light through its edge or body. Onyx is the most translucent natural stone, making it the premier choice for backlit installations.
catalogue.glossary_veining_desc: Natural mineral bands, streaks, or fractures in stone, created by geological pressure and mineral deposition over millions of years. Distinct from structural fissures.
catalogue.glossary_fissure_desc: A natural hairline crack within stone, caused by geological stress. Unlike a crack from impact, fissures are inherent to the material and do not compromise structural integrity.
catalogue.ref_sourcing_lead: Every stone in this catalogue is sourced directly from quarries we know personally. Our team visits each site to evaluate colour consistency, block quality, and extraction practices before any stone is shipped.
datasheet.spec_slip_note: Increase with honed, leathered, brushed, or textured finishes
datasheet.footer_address: DIJA Natural Stone — 10031 Sok. No:14, AOSB, Çiğli 35620 — İzmir, Türkiye
datasheet.format_custom_desc: Custom sizes available to meet your project specifications
datasheet.limit_marble_chemical: Sensitive to acidic substances — lemon, vinegar, wine, cola, and most cleaning chemicals cause etching on contact. Etching is most visible on polished finishes. Honed and leather finishes are more forgiving.
datasheet.limit_marble_freeze: Moderate freeze-thaw resistance. Suitable for exterior use in mild climates with proper sealing. Not recommended for areas with frequent freeze-thaw cycles.
datasheet.limit_marble_stain: Moderate porosity (absorption 0.1–0.5%). Requires annual sealing for stain protection. Oily substances should be wiped immediately.
datasheet.limit_marble_heat: Good heat resistance. Can tolerate hot cookware for short periods. Thermal shock may cause cracking — always use trivets.
datasheet.limit_marble_slip: Polished marble is slippery when wet — unsuitable for pool surrounds and wet areas. Honed or textured finishes improve slip resistance.
datasheet.limit_marble_maint: Seal annually. Clean with pH-neutral stone cleaner only. Never use vinegar, bleach, or ammonia-based products.
datasheet.limit_onyx_chemical: Extremely sensitive to acids and chemicals. Surface damage occurs almost instantly on contact. Only water or specialised stone soap should be used.
datasheet.limit_onyx_freeze: Not suitable for exterior applications. Onyx is too soft and fragile for freeze-thaw environments.
datasheet.limit_onyx_stain: High porosity — requires thorough sealing. Stains easily from any coloured liquid. Not recommended for food preparation areas.
datasheet.limit_onyx_heat: Moderate heat resistance but prone to thermal shock cracking. Use trivets and coasters at all times.
datasheet.limit_onyx_slip: Polished onyx is very slippery when wet. Reserve for vertical surfaces and decorative accents only.
datasheet.limit_onyx_maint: Professional sealing recommended. Clean with soft cloth and pH-neutral cleaner. Avoid all abrasives.
datasheet.limit_lime_chemical: Highly sensitive to acids. Etches and pits easily even from mild acidic liquids (fruit juice, coffee). Not recommended for kitchen countertops.
datasheet.limit_lime_freeze: Moderate freeze-thaw resistance. Dense limestones perform better. Best suited for mild climates when used externally.
datasheet.limit_lime_stain: Porous material — requires impregnating sealer. Stains readily from oils, wine, and organic matter. Darker colours are more forgiving.
datasheet.limit_lime_heat: Natural thermal insulator — stays cool in summer, retains warmth in winter. Good for radiant floor heating. Heat resistant but avoid direct flame.
datasheet.limit_lime_slip: Honed, tumbled, or textured finishes provide good slip resistance. Suitable for pool surrounds when appropriately finished.
datasheet.limit_lime_maint: Seal before first use and reapply annually. Use only pH-neutral cleaners. Avoid wax-based products that yellow over time.
datasheet.limit_trav_chemical: Sensitive to acidic substances. Filled travertine is somewhat more resistant, but unfilled travertine traps acidic liquids in its natural voids.
datasheet.limit_trav_freeze: Good freeze-thaw resistance. Traditional building stone of the Mediterranean — performs well in exterior applications. Suitable for pool surrounds.
datasheet.limit_trav_stain: Naturally porous with surface voids. Filled travertine resists staining better. Unfilled voids trap dirt and require more maintenance.
datasheet.limit_trav_heat: Excellent heat resistance. Natural insulator — stays cool underfoot. Traditional choice for radiant heated floors.
datasheet.limit_trav_slip: Unfilled or tumbled travertine offers high slip resistance. Filled and polished surfaces can be slippery. Choose finish based on application.
datasheet.limit_trav_maint: Seal filled travertine annually. Unfilled travertine requires periodic deep cleaning of voids. Professional maintenance recommended.
datasheet.limit_gran_chemical: Excellent acid resistance — one of the most chemically durable natural stones. Does not etch from kitchen acids. Suitable for all food preparation areas.
datasheet.limit_gran_freeze: Excellent freeze-thaw resistance. Suitable for all exterior applications including paving, cladding, and pool surrounds in any climate.
datasheet.limit_gran_stain: Low porosity when sealed — excellent stain resistance. Annual sealing recommended but many granites are dense enough to resist staining without it.
datasheet.limit_gran_heat: Excellent heat resistance. Can withstand hot pans directly on surface. Extremely resistant to thermal shock. Ideal for kitchen countertops.
datasheet.limit_gran_slip: Honed, flamed, or bush-hammered finishes provide excellent slip resistance for outdoor use. Polished granite is moderately slippery when wet.
datasheet.limit_gran_maint: Seal every 1–2 years. Clean with mild dish soap and water. Resistant to most household chemicals. Minimal maintenance required.
datasheet.limit_qtzt_chemical: Very good acid resistance — comparable to granite. Does not etch from kitchen acids. Suitable for all food preparation surfaces.
datasheet.limit_qtzt_freeze: Excellent freeze-thaw resistance. Extremely durable in all climates. Suitable for all exterior applications.
datasheet.limit_qtzt_stain: Very low porosity — excellent stain resistance when sealed. One of the least porous natural stones. Annual sealing is sufficient.
datasheet.limit_qtzt_heat: Excellent heat resistance. Hardness (Mohs 7) makes it resistant to scratching and thermal damage. Ideal for heavy-use surfaces.
datasheet.limit_qtzt_slip: Polished quartzite can be slippery when wet. Honed or textured finishes are recommended for wet areas and exterior applications.
datasheet.limit_qtzt_maint: Seal annually. Clean with pH-neutral stone cleaner. Very durable and low-maintenance — similar to granite in daily care.

privacy.privacy_1: DIJA Natural Stone respects your privacy. This policy explains how we collect, use, and protect your personal data when you visit our website or use our services.
privacy.privacy_2: We collect only the information you provide through our contact forms: name, email address, phone number, and project details. We also collect basic analytics data through server logs.
privacy.privacy_3: Your data is used solely to respond to your inquiries, provide quotes, and improve our website. We do not sell, rent, or share your data with third parties for marketing purposes.
privacy.privacy_4: Your data is stored securely and retained only as long as necessary to fulfill your request. You may request deletion of your data at any time by contacting us.
privacy.privacy_5: For questions about this policy, email info@dijastones.com.
privacy.terms_1: By using the DIJA Natural Stone website, you agree to these terms. All content, images, and text on this website are the property of DIJA Natural Stone unless otherwise noted.
privacy.terms_2: Product images are for illustrative purposes. Natural stone varies in color, veining, and texture. We recommend viewing physical samples before ordering.
privacy.terms_3: All prices and availability are subject to change without notice. Quotes are valid for 14 days unless otherwise stated.
privacy.cookies_1: This website uses minimal cookies. We use session cookies for form functionality and basic analytics to understand how visitors use our site.
privacy.cookies_2: We do not use tracking cookies, advertising cookies, or third-party marketing cookies. No personal data is collected through cookies.
privacy.cookies_3: You can disable cookies in your browser settings, though this may affect certain functionality.

home.hero.title:
A quarry is just
the earth's library,
home.hero.subtitle: and we know exactly which shelf to browse.
home.why_title:
A house built on
geology and patience.
home.why_text: We source directly from family-owned quarries across Turkey, the Mediterranean, Brazil, and India — no middlemen, no shortcuts. Just traceable stone, fairly priced, personally vetted.
home.why_text2: From a single slab for a private residence to multi-container orders for hotel developments — we deliver 152 natural stones to 47 countries, with the same standard: the right stone, accurately described, delivered on time.
home.featured_text: A monolithic retreat on the Sardinian coast, sheathed entirely in honed Thassos White. 84 tons of stone, every panel book-matched by hand.
home.process_01_text: We partner with family-owned quarries across the Mediterranean, Brazil, and India, selecting blocks that meet our standards for tone, figure, and integrity.
home.process_02_text: Gang saws slice blocks into slabs along the vein's preferred axis. Master cutters study each face before committing the blade.
home.process_03_text: Honed, polished, leathered, brushed, bush-hammered — the finish decides how stone meets light.
home.process_04_text: Custom crates, climate-controlled containers, and a logistics team that has delivered to 47 countries without a single broken slab.
home.quote_text: "DIJA doesn't sell stone. They sell the silence of a mountain that has been listening to the sea for two thousand years."
incoterm.exw.name: Ex Works
incoterm.exw.description: Buyer collects goods from our atelier in Izmir, Turkey. Buyer assumes all costs and risks from our door onward.
incoterm.fca.name: Free Carrier
incoterm.fca.description: Seller delivers goods to the carrier or another person nominated by buyer at seller's premises or another named place.
incoterm.fas.name: Free Alongside Ship
incoterm.fas.description: Seller delivers goods alongside the vessel at the port of loading. Buyer assumes all costs and risks from that point.
incoterm.fob.name: Free on Board
incoterm.fob.description: Seller delivers goods on board the vessel nominated by buyer at the port of loading. Buyer assumes all costs and risks once goods are on board.
incoterm.cfr.name: Cost and Freight
incoterm.cfr.description: Seller covers the cost and freight to bring goods to the destination port. Risk transfers to buyer once goods are on board at origin.
incoterm.cif.name: Cost, Insurance & Freight
incoterm.cif.description: Seller covers cost, insurance, and freight to the destination port. Risk transfers to buyer once goods are on board at origin.
incoterm.cpt.name: Carriage Paid To
incoterm.cpt.description: Seller pays carriage to the named destination. Risk transfers to buyer when goods are handed to the first carrier.
incoterm.cip.name: Carriage & Insurance Paid To
incoterm.cip.description: Seller pays carriage and insurance to the named destination. Risk transfers to buyer when goods are handed to the first carrier.
incoterm.dap.name: Delivered at Place
incoterm.dap.description: Seller delivers goods to the buyer's named place of destination. Seller bears all risks and costs up to delivery (excluding import clearance).
incoterm.ddp.name: Delivered Duty Paid
incoterm.ddp.description: Seller delivers goods to the buyer's named place, cleared for import, with all duties and taxes paid. Maximum seller obligation.
payment.tt_30_70.name: T/T 30% Advance + 70% Before Shipment
payment.tt_30_70.description: 30% of the total invoice value is paid by bank wire transfer as a down payment upon proforma confirmation. The remaining 70% is paid before the goods leave our warehouse / port of loading.
payment.tt_30_70.requirements: Standard for new clients. Proforma valid for 14 days.
payment.lc_sight.name: Confirmed Irrevocable L/C at Sight
payment.lc_sight.description: Confirmed, irrevocable, and transferable Letter of Credit payable at sight. The full amount is paid upon presentation of shipping documents (bill of lading, commercial invoice, packing list).
payment.lc_sight.requirements: L/C must be confirmed by a prime bank acceptable to DIJA. Valid for 60 days after issuance.
payment.cad.name: Cash Against Documents (C.A.D.)
payment.cad.description: Buyer pays the full invoice amount at the bank of the seller's agent in exchange for the original shipping documents (bill of lading, commercial invoice, packing list, certificate of origin).
payment.cad.requirements: Available only after export manager confirmation and established relationship. Subject to credit evaluation.

col.quiet-whites.d: Curated for minimalist residential and hospitality projects.
col.deep-seas.d: For accent walls and surfaces that feel like deep water.
col.warm-earths.d: For terraces, floors, and surfaces touched by bare feet.
col.veined-drama.d: For the single statement surface — island, fireplace, feature wall.
col.earth-fire.d: The warm-toned stones of the Mediterranean basin.
col.exotic-statement.d: When the brief calls for something nobody's seen before.
catalogue.type_marble_desc: Marble is a metamorphic limestone composed of calcite. Its crystalline structure and luminous veining give it an unmatched ability to capture and reflect light. Some varieties are dolomitic — composed of magnesium carbonate — offering greater hardness and improved resistance to etching.
catalogue.type_onyx_desc: Onyx is a banded, translucent calcite stone formed in cave systems and mineral springs. Its layered structure and luminous glow make it the most dramatic stone for backlit and feature applications.
catalogue.type_limestone_desc: Limestone is a sedimentary stone formed from ancient marine deposits. Its warm, matte surface and subtle fossil patterns bring an earthy, Mediterranean character to any space.
catalogue.type_travertine_desc: Travertine is a terrestrial limestone deposited by mineral-rich spring waters. Its cellular cavities and layered banding create a textured surface unlike any other natural stone.
catalogue.type_granite_desc: Granite is an igneous rock formed from cooled magma deep within the earth. Its dense crystalline structure of quartz and feldspar makes it one of the hardest natural stones available.
catalogue.type_quartzite_desc: Quartzite is a metamorphic rock formed when sandstone recrystallizes under extreme heat and pressure. It rivals granite in hardness while offering veining reminiscent of marble.
catalogue.marble_best: Wall cladding, bathroom vanities, low-traffic flooring, fireplace surrounds, decorative surfaces
catalogue.marble_consider: Etches on contact with acids — citrus, wine, and vinegar can leave permanent marks. Scratches more readily than granite. Not recommended for kitchen countertops or heavy commercial traffic.
catalogue.marble_care: Sealing is required. Honed or leather finishes conceal everyday wear far better than polished. Use only pH-neutral cleaners. Avoid abrasive pads.
catalogue.onyx_best: Backlit wall cladding, bar tops, bathroom vanities, feature walls, decorative panels, reception desks
catalogue.onyx_consider: Soft and fragile — scratches, chips, and cracks easily. Translucency varies by slab thickness (2 cm backlit optimally). Not suitable for flooring, kitchen countertops, or any high-traffic application.
catalogue.onyx_care: Handle with extreme care during fabrication. Always backlight-test slabs before installation. Seal thoroughly. Clean only with soft cloth and pH-neutral cleaner. Never use abrasive materials.
catalogue.limestone_best: Indoor and outdoor flooring, wall cladding, facades, paving, landscape features, feature walls
catalogue.limestone_consider: Softer and more porous than marble. Stains easily without thorough sealing. Not recommended for kitchen worktops or wet areas unless properly treated and maintained.
catalogue.limestone_care: Requires thorough sealing, especially for outdoor use. Tumbled and aged finishes naturally hide wear. Re-seal periodically — frequency depends on traffic and exposure.
catalogue.travertine_best: Indoor flooring (filled finish), pool surrounds and outdoor paving (unfilled), wall cladding, facades
catalogue.travertine_consider: Softer than marble — can scratch and chip under heavy impact. Unfilled cavities trap dirt and require more maintenance. Highly porous; diligent sealing essential in wet areas.
catalogue.travertine_care: Filled finish for smooth interior surfaces. Unfilled for rustic outdoor character. Sealing is non-negotiable in wet areas. Avoid acidic or abrasive cleaners.
catalogue.granite_best: Kitchen countertops, exterior cladding, high-traffic flooring, paving, staircases, monuments
catalogue.granite_consider: Very heavy — verify structural support before specifying. Can still stain despite low porosity. Some granites contain trace minerals that may rust or weather differently outdoors.
catalogue.granite_care: Lower maintenance than marble. Sealing recommended but less frequent. Excellent heat and scratch resistance. Avoid harsh chemical descalers.
catalogue.quartzite_best: Kitchen countertops, high-traffic flooring, wall cladding, bathroom vanities
catalogue.quartzite_consider: Some varieties can be brittle and prone to cracking during fabrication. Not all quartzites are equally hard — test before specifying. Generally more expensive than granite.
catalogue.quartzite_care: Sealing recommended. Very durable once installed. Resists etching from kitchen acids far better than marble. Honed finish recommended for heavy-use surfaces.
catalogue.marble_finishes: Polished, Honed, Leather, Brushed, Sandblasted, Tumbled, Bush-hammered
catalogue.limestone_finishes: Honed, Brushed, Tumbled, Split-face, Sandblasted, Bush-hammered, Aged, Polished
catalogue.travertine_finishes: Filled & Honed, Filled & Polished, Unfilled & Tumbled, Unfilled & Brushed, Cross-cut, Vein-cut
catalogue.granite_finishes: Polished, Honed, Leather, Flamed, Brushed, Sandblasted, Bush-hammered
catalogue.marble_apps: Interior wall cladding, bathroom vanities, low-traffic flooring, decorative surfaces, fireplace surrounds
catalogue.onyx_apps: Backlit wall cladding, bar tops, bathroom vanities, feature walls, decorative panels, reception desks
catalogue.limestone_apps: Indoor and outdoor flooring, wall cladding, paving, landscape design, feature walls, facades
catalogue.travertine_apps: Indoor flooring, wall cladding, pool surrounds, outdoor paving, facades, decorative features
catalogue.granite_apps: Kitchen countertops, exterior cladding, high-traffic flooring, paving, staircases, monuments
catalogue.quartzite_apps: Kitchen countertops, high-traffic flooring, wall cladding, bathroom vanities, luxury surfaces
catalogue.marble_note: Marble offers excellent thermal conductivity — feels cool underfoot, ideal for radiant heating systems. Sensitive to acidic substances; honed finishes recommended for kitchen countertops.
catalogue.onyx_note: Onyx is prized for its translucency — always specify backlighting to reveal its full character. Extremely soft; reserve for vertical surfaces and low-traffic decorative use only.
catalogue.limestone_note: Limestone provides natural thermal insulation — remains cool in summer and retains warmth in winter, ideal for radiant floor heating. A softer, porous stone; requires sealing for wet areas. Not recommended for kitchen countertops.
catalogue.travertine_note: Available filled (smooth surface, ideal for interiors) or unfilled (natural pitted texture, ideal for rustic and outdoor applications).
catalogue.granite_note: Granite is one of the hardest natural stones — resistant to scratches, heat, and stains when sealed. Ideal for kitchen countertops and high-traffic commercial flooring.
catalogue.quartzite_note: Quartzite offers marble-like veining with granite-level hardness (Mohs ~7). Excellent for kitchen countertops, backsplashes, and high-use surfaces.
catalogue.fallback_type_desc: A natural stone prized for its beauty, durability, and timeless character. Each piece carries the unique geological story of its origin.
catalogue.finish_polished_desc: High-gloss, mirror-like shine achieved by fine-grit abrasion. Reflects light, emphasises colour and veining.
catalogue.finish_honed_desc: Matte to satin finish with low light reflection. Conceals wear and etching better than polished.
catalogue.finish_leather_desc: Slightly textured, low-sheen finish with a tactile surface. Between honed and brushed in texture.
catalogue.finish_brushed_desc: Textured surface created by rotating nylon brushes. Softens the stone while preserving natural colour.
catalogue.finish_flamed_desc: Rough texture produced by high-temperature flame. Highly slip-resistant, ideal for exterior use.
catalogue.finish_tumbled_desc: Weathered, aged appearance achieved by tumbling with abrasives. Softens edges and creates an antique look.
catalogue.finish_bush_hammered_desc: Patterned texture created by a pneumatic hammer. Provides grip and a rustic appearance.
catalogue.finish_sandblasted_desc: Uniform matte texture created by high-pressure sand. Reduces surface gloss evenly.
catalogue.finish_aged_desc: Artificial or natural weathering that softens edges and creates a time-worn patina.
catalogue.finish_split_face_desc: Natural fracture surface, rough and irregular. Used for dramatic wall cladding.
catalogue.finish_filled_honed_desc: Travertine cavities filled with cement/resin, then honed to a smooth satin surface.
catalogue.finish_filled_polished_desc: Travertine cavities filled and polished to a glossy, seamless surface.
catalogue.finish_cross_cut_desc: Travertine cut perpendicular to bedding. Shows filled cavities as small dots; uniform appearance.
catalogue.finish_vein_cut_desc: Travertine cut parallel to bedding. Emphasises natural banding and open cavities.
